dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for a priority 1 assault in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Matt, an EMT with Rescue Unit 7. What made you call 911 today?
We found a man who was just assaulted at the bar. He isn't responding to us.
We need to check his responsiveness. Is he conscious in any way at all?
Not seeing any eye movement. Eyes are closed and not reactive.
Mr. Smith, can you hear me? No response. He doesn't react.
Gently tapping his shoulder-no movement. No motor response detected.
Airway appears patent with no obstruction. He's not in distress.
Breathing is spontaneous, regular, and measured at 20 per minute.
Pulse is regular, easily palpable. Skin is warm, slightly clammy, and pink.
Skin has normal turgor and signs of a minor abrasion on the posterior left scalp. Minor bleeding noted.
Head and neck appear atraumatic except for a small abrasion on the left posterior scalp. Pupils are PEARL at 3 mm.
No obstruction or foreign body in ears, nose, or mouth. Pupillary reflex intact.
Bystanders say the patient was struck in the head and face with a closed fist then fell and hit his head on the concrete. Lost consciousness immediately.
Pulse is 53-steady and regular.
Respiratory rate is 20-normal.
Blood pressure is 142 over 88 mmHg.
SpO2 is 96 percent-within acceptable range.
Blood glucose is 123 mg/dL-no hypoglycemia signs.
EKG shows sinus bradycardia with a generally normal rhythm.
Chest is symmetric and shows no signs of trauma or deformity.
No abnormalities in the abdomen or pelvis. No rigidity or tenderness found.
Extremities and back show no deformities or wounds. Patient moved freely on stretcher.
Applying a c-collar for spinal precautions due to blunt head trauma mechanism.
Starting an 18-gauge IV in the left forearm. Easy access and no complications.
Placing an ETCO2 nasal cannula. Monitors for normal breathing activity.
Managing per protocol 4-1 for general trauma based on field findings.
Heart rate is variable per the monitor although stable otherwise.
Patient exhibits jaw clenching and minor combativeness.
No signs of pain. Glasgow Coma Scale is at 3-no motor, verbal, or eye response.
Transporting to VCU Medical Center. The facility is a trauma center.
Preparing to move the patient onto the Reeves stretcher for transport.
Rechecking vitals. Blood pressure is 142/88, pulse 53, respirations 20, SpO2 96 percent.
Finishing protocol 4-1 with no new developments.
Rechecks pulse rate-still at 53, steady, and well-tolerated.
Documenting periods of jaw clenching and slight combativeness-present despite unresponsiveness.
Unable to determine the patient's last meal or fluids via bystanders.
All medication, allergy, and medical history could not be completed due to unconscious state.
No new wounds or abrasions. Scalp abrasion stopped bleeding.
EKG remains stable-no new arrhythmia or abnormality observed.
Ready to hand over the patient. He remains unconscious, with GCS of 3 and signs of blunt head trauma.
Patient is secured and ready for transport.
Glucose remains at 123-within normal range with no signs of concern.
Patient is a 30-year-old male with a normal weight of 75 kg.
Patient transferred in the stretcher via standard recline locks and straps.
No abnormal breath sounds. Breathing stable and regular.
Breathing remains regular with no signs of distress or accessory muscle use.
Preparing for ED handover with all documentation and monitoring logs.
Patient has blunt head trauma, GCS 3, sinus bradycardia, and a minor scalp abrasion.
Stretcher and monitors secured. Unit is ready for immediate transport.
